title: LCK-I
text: Which of the following is a primary goal of Encapsulation?
choices: Allowing any class to modify internal data directly.
Bundle data and methods, restrict access
Making sure a class has only one instance.
Speeding up the execution of mathematical loops.

title: LCK-II
text: In Java, which keyword is primarily used to implement the principle of Inheritance?
choices: implements
static
extends
encapsulate

title: LCK-III
text: Which scenario best demonstrates Polymorphism?
choices: One Shape var holds Circle, Square, or Triangle
Hiding the password variable in a User class.
Running a program on multiple operating systems.
Using a for loop to iterate through a list.

title: LCK-IV
text: Which of these is an example of Abstraction in a Java program?
choices: Writing a very long method with 100 lines of code.
Interface defines behavior without implementation
Using the private keyword on all variables.
Declaring an array with a fixed size.

title: LCK-V
text: What is the relationship between a Class and an Object?
choices: An object is a template used to create a class.
A class is an instance of an object in memory.
Class is blueprint; object is instance
There is no difference; they are synonyms in Java.

title: LCK-VI
text: To implement Method Overloading, what must be true?
choices: The method names must be different.
Same name methods with different params
Overloaded methods must be in same class
Different return types with same params

title: LCK-VII
text: When a child class provides a specific implementation for a method that is already defined in its parent class, this is known as:
choices: Method Overloading
Method Overriding
Method Redundancy
Method Extraction

title: LCK-VIII
text: If a variable is declared as static in Java, what does it mean?
choices: Each object has its own copy of the variable
The variable's value cannot be changed.
The variable is shared across all instances of the class.
The variable can only be accessed within the main method.

title: LCK-IX
text: Why is OOP (Object-Oriented Programming) considered beneficial for large software projects?
choices: It eliminates the need for testing.
It promotes modular code for easier debugging
It allows code to run without a Java Virtual Machine (JVM).
It converts all text into binary automatically.

title: LCK-X
text: What is a key characteristic of a Constructor in Java?
choices: Constructor name matches class and has no return type
It is used to delete objects from memory.
It must be declared as void.
It is only called when a method is overloaded.

title: LCK-XI
text: The this keyword is most commonly used to:
choices: Call a method from a different class.
Differentiate attributes from parameters
Access the private variables of a parent class.
Terminate a loop early.

title: LCK-XII
text: Why would a developer use Generics, such as ArrayList<String>?
choices: Allow list to store any data type
Restrict list to specific type for safety
To make the code run on older versions of Java.
To automatically sort the data in the list.

title: LCK-XIII
text: If a variable is marked as "protected," which of the following groups is EXCLUDED from accessing it?
choices: Any class that exists within the same package.
Any subclass of the parent class
A completely unrelated class located in a different package.
The class in which the variable was originally defined.

title: LCK-XIV
text: What is the most likely cause of a StackOverflowError in a Java application?
choices: A recursive method that never reaches a base case.
Trying to store a very large string in a variable.
Running out of RAM on the computer.
A while loop that runs over thousand times.

title: LCK-XV
text: Regarding the "rules of inheritance," how do Interfaces and Abstract Classes differ in how they can be used by a single Java class?
choices: Extend one abstract class but implement many interfaces
Can't extend multiple abstract classes
Don't have to implement every package interface
Extending and implementing follow different rules

title: LCK-XVI
text: Which statement correctly describes the use of throw?
choices: Declare exceptions in method signature
Use to throw an exception in code
It is used to catch an exception after it happens.
It is used to prevent any exceptions from occurring.

title: LCK-XVII
text: What is considered a "bad practice" when handling exceptions?
choices: Using multiple catch blocks for different error types.
Using a finally block to close resources.
Catching an exception and leaving the catch block empty.
Throwing a custom exception.

title: LCK-XVIII
text: What does the 'super' keyword do in Java?
choices: Refers to parent class for access
It creates a new instance of the current class.
It marks a method as final and prevents overriding.
It terminates the current method execution.

title: LCK-XIX
text: Which access modifier allows access only within the same class?
choices: public
protected
private
default

title: LCK-XX
text: Which access modifier allows access within the same package and subclasses?
choices: public
protected
private
default

title: LCK-XXI
text: What is the default access level (package-private) in Java?
choices: Accessible only within the same class
Accessible within the same package
Accessible from anywhere
Never accessible

title: LCK-XXII
text: What access modifier allows access from anywhere in the application?
choices: public
protected
private
default

title: LCK-XXIII
text: What is the main purpose of the 'super()' call in a constructor?
choices: To call the parent class's constructor.
To create a new instance of the current class.
To call a method from the current class.
To initialize static variables.

title: LCK-XXIV
text: How can you explicitly call a parent class method from a child class?
choices: Using the 'parent' keyword
Using the 'super' keyword
Using the 'extends' keyword
Using the 'this' keyword

title: LCK-XXV
text: What does the 'new' keyword do in Java?
choices: It declares a new variable.
It allocates memory for an object and calls its constructor.
It creates a new class.
It imports a new package.

title: LCK-XXVI
text: What happens when you create an object using the 'new' keyword?
choices: A variable is declared and memory is allocated on the stack
Memory is allocated on the heap and the constructor is called
A reference is created but no memory is allocated
The garbage collector is invoked

title: LCK-XXVII
text: What does it mean for a method to be 'abstract'?
choices: It contains no implementation and must be overridden in subclasses.
It cannot be called from other classes.
It has multiple implementations in the same class.
It is a method that is automatically generated.

title: LCK-XXVIII
text: What can contain abstract methods in Java?
choices: Only classes
Abstract classes and interfaces
Concrete classes and interfaces
Only regular methods

title: LCK-XXIX
text: Can you instantiate an abstract class directly?
choices: Yes, always
No, you must create a subclass that implements all abstract methods
Yes, but only with parameters
Only if it has a main method

title: LCK-XXX
text: What does the 'interface' keyword define in Java?
choices: A contract that classes must follow by implementing its methods.
A class with only abstract methods.
A way to inherit from multiple classes.
A package of related classes.

title: LCK-XXXI
text: Can a class implement multiple interfaces in Java?
choices: No, a class can implement only one interface
Yes, a class can implement multiple interfaces
Only if the interfaces are related
Only in Java 8 and later

title: LCK-XXXII
text: What is the 'default' method in an interface used for?
choices: To provide a default constructor.
To provide a default implementation of a method that implementing classes can override.
To define methods that must be implemented.
To create private methods in an interface.

title: LCK-XXXIII
text: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of an interface in Java?
choices: It can declare abstract methods.
It can contain constructor implementations.
A class can implement multiple interfaces.
It can declare constants (static final variables).

title: LCK-XXXIV
text: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface?
choices: They are identical in functionality.
A class can extend only one abstract class, but implement multiple interfaces.
Interfaces can have constructors; abstract classes cannot.
Abstract classes allow multiple inheritance; interfaces do not.

title: LCK-XXXV
text: Can an interface extend another interface in Java?
choices: No, interfaces cannot extend other interfaces
Yes, an interface can extend another interface
Only if they have the same methods
Only in Java 9 and later

title: LCK-XXXVI
text: Which statement is true about the 'finally' block?
choices: It is optional and executes only if an exception occurs.
It always executes, whether an exception occurs or not.
It must contain exception handling code.
It is executed before the try block.

title: LCK-XXXVII
text: What is the correct order of exception handling in a try-catch-finally block?
choices: finally executes first, then catch, then the rest of the code.
try executes first, then catch (if needed), then finally always executes.
catch executes first, then try, then finally.
finally executes first, then the rest depends on exceptions.

title: LCK-XXXVIII
text: What is the difference between a checked and an unchecked exception?
choices: Checked exceptions must be caught or declared; unchecked exceptions don't need to.
Unchecked exceptions are more severe than checked exceptions.
Both require the same handling in code.
There is no difference between them.

title: LCK-XXXIX
text: Which of the following is a checked exception?
choices: NullPointerException
IOException
ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
ArithmeticException

title: LCK-XL
text: What does the 'throws' keyword specify in a method signature?
choices: The exceptions that the method may throw and not handle internally.
The exceptions that must be caught by the caller.
The exceptions that the method will definitely throw.
The same thing as the 'throw' keyword.

title: LCK-XLI
text: What is a 'null pointer exception' in Java?
choices: An exception thrown when accessing a method of a null object.
An exception thrown when declaring a variable.
An exception that occurs when a pointer is out of bounds.
An exception that prevents null values from being used.

title: LCK-XLII
text: What is the main purpose of using 'try-with-resources' in Java?
choices: To automatically allocate memory for objects.
To automatically close resources (like file streams) after use.
To prevent exceptions from being thrown.
To create multiple instances of the same resource.

title: LCK-XLIII
text: What is the main purpose of the 'finally' block in exception handling?
choices: To handle all types of exceptions.
To execute code that must run regardless of whether an exception occurred.
To prevent exceptions from being thrown.
To retry the code in the try block.

title: LCK-XLIV
text: What is the purpose of the 'Collection' framework in Java?
choices: To provide a unified way to store and manipulate groups of objects.
To replace arrays entirely.
To handle only primitive data types.
To manage file I/O operations.

title: LCK-XLV
text: What is the difference between a List and a Set in Java?
choices: A List allows duplicates and maintains order; a Set does not allow duplicates.
A Set allows duplicates; a List does not.
They are identical.
A List is faster than a Set.

title: LCK-XLVI
text: What is the main difference between ArrayList and LinkedList in Java?
choices: ArrayList is faster for access; LinkedList is faster for insertions/deletions.
LinkedList is faster for access than ArrayList.
They have identical performance characteristics.
ArrayList uses linked nodes; LinkedList uses arrays.

title: LCK-XLVII
text: What is the primary difference between HashMap and Hashtable in Java?
choices: HashMap is synchronized; Hashtable is not.
Hashtable is synchronized; HashMap is not.
They have the same functionality.
HashMap can store null keys; Hashtable cannot.

title: LCK-XLVIII
text: What data structure does HashMap use internally?
choices: Linked list
Array of buckets with hash function
Tree structure
Graph structure

title: LCK-XLIX
text: Can HashMap store null keys and values?
choices: No, neither keys nor values can be null
Yes, one null key and multiple null values are allowed
Only null values are allowed, not keys
Only null keys are allowed, not values

title: LCK-L
text: What is the relationship between the 'Comparable' and 'Comparator' interfaces?
choices: They are identical and can be used interchangeably.
'Comparable' defines a single natural ordering; 'Comparator' allows multiple custom orderings.
'Comparator' is deprecated in favor of 'Comparable'.
'Comparable' is used for primitive types; 'Comparator' is for objects.

title: LCK-LI
text: What is the purpose of the 'Comparable' interface?
choices: To define a way for objects to compare themselves to other objects.
To compare primitive data types.
To prevent comparison between objects.
To define constants in a class.

title: LCK-LII
text: What method must be implemented by a class that implements Comparable?
choices: compare()
compareTo()
equals()
sort()

title: LCK-LIII
text: How many comparators can be used for sorting a collection?
choices: Only one comparator per collection
Multiple custom comparators can be defined and used
Comparators cannot be used with collections
Only the default comparator can be used

title: LCK-LIV
text: What is an 'enum' in Java?
choices: A data type that can hold a fixed set of constant values.
A class with no methods.
A list of variables.
A way to create interfaces.

title: LCK-LV
text: Can an enum implement an interface in Java?
choices: No, enums cannot implement interfaces
Yes, an enum can implement one or more interfaces
Only if the interface has no methods
Only in Java 10 and later

title: LCK-LVI
text: Can an enum have methods and variables?
choices: No, enums can only contain constants
Yes, enums can have methods, variables, and constructors
Only methods are allowed, not variables
Only in Java 12 and later

title: LCK-LVII
text: What is Generics in Java primarily used for?
choices: To create classes without specific types
To provide compile-time type safety and eliminate casting
To allow unlimited type combinations
To replace inheritance in Java

title: LCK-LVIII
text: What is the syntax for declaring a generic class in Java?
choices: class MyClass { }
class MyClass<T> { }
class MyClass(T) { }
class MyClass[T] { }

title: LCK-LIX
text: What does the 'T' represent in a generic class like 'Box<T>'?
choices: A specific data type like String or Integer
A type parameter that can be replaced with any type
Always represents the type 'T'
A primitive type only

title: LCK-LX
text: Can you use primitive types as type parameters in generics?
choices: Yes, primitives work directly with generics
No, you must use wrapper classes like Integer, Double, etc.
Only for the Integer type
Only in Java 15 and later

title: LCK-LXI
text: What is a bounded type parameter in Java generics?
choices: A type parameter with no restrictions
A type parameter restricted to a specific type or its subtypes
A parameter that can only be String
A parameter that has a maximum size

title: LCK-LXII
text: How do you create an instance of a generic class with a specific type?
choices: Box box = new Box();
Box<String> box = new Box<String>();
Box<String> box = new Box();
Box box = new Box<String>();

title: LCK-LXIII
text: What is the purpose of wildcard '?' in Java generics?
choices: To represent any specific type
To allow flexibility when the specific type is unknown or irrelevant
To create a new generic type
To represent null types

title: LCK-LXIV
text: What does List<?> represent in Java?
choices: A list of any type
A list of type 'Object' only
An empty list
A list of null values

title: LCK-LXV
text: What is the difference between '? extends T' and '? super T' in generics?
choices: 'extends' restricts to T and its subclasses; 'super' restricts to T and its superclasses
They have the same functionality
'extends' is only for classes; 'super' is only for interfaces
'super' restricts to T only
